messages.content:
\section{Introduction}
As the parameter counts and data volumes of Large Language Models (LLMs) continue to expand, relying solely on scaling these dimensions is becoming increasingly expensive. Consequently, a new wave of models, represented by OpenAI-o1 and DeepSeek-R1, has pivoted towards exploring test-time compute, which allocates increased inference time to tackle intricate reasoning tasks. Drawing inspiration from Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), methods based on Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) have emerged as a mainstream paradigm for enhancing LLM reasoning.
Furthermore, to extrapolate this reasoning capability to more sophisticated scenarios, a significant portion of research is focusing on enabling models to leverage external tools and environmental feedback. Empowered by the robust reasoning capabilities of LLMs, agent systems designed for interactive tasks, such as web search and automated machine learning model training, have demonstrated substantial application potential.
In discussions regarding the reasoning capabilities of LLMs, test-time compute stands as a pivotal concept. Prior to the widespread adoption of LLMs in tool-use scenarios, test-time compute in standard reasoning tasks (such as mathematics, coding, and commonsense reasoning) was typically directly correlated with generation length. Intuitively, in scenarios involving standalone model inference, an inherent positive correlation exists between generation time and generation length.
However, this relationship breaks down entirely when discussing agentic scenarios characterized by complex tool utilization. In such settings, since the actual latency of tool execution is unpredictable, the total time required to complete a task cannot be estimated solely based on the model's generation length. A concise reasoning trajectory might consume a significant amount of time if it involves high-latency tools. Conversely, a more verbose generation could end faster given rapid environmental feedback. Consequently, the traditional definition of test-time compute based on generation length proves inapplicable for agentic tasks.
In this work, we introduce the concept of the \textbf{\textsc{Timely Machine}}. By decoupling test time from generation tokens, we restore the concept of "test time" to its original meaning as a physical quantity:  \textbf{time itself}. We argue that the awareness of task duration and the ability to plan for task costs should be intrinsic capabilities of LLMs in agentic scenarios. Models should be capable of dynamically adjusting their generation strategies in real-time, based on the remaining time budget and feedback from the current environment.
Specifically, when environmental feedback incurs high latency, the model should appropriately adjust its interaction frequency and prioritize the quality of each interaction round to achieve better results within the fixed time limit; conversely, when the environment responds rapidly, the model should engage in fast, multi-round interactions to gather more sufficient feedback, thereby exploring for higher payoffs within the same duration. Only by being trained to possess sensitivity to the physical quantity of "time" can models truly break free from the artificial constraint of "generation length," allowing them to fully utilize the expanded test time budget in agentic scenarios.
To validate this capability, we introduce \textbf{Timely-Eval}, a benchmark designed to evaluate how well models adapt their strategies based on temporal feedback. Within Timely-Eval, we curate three distinct interactive scenarios: high-frequency and multi-turn tool invocation with environmental feedback, low-frequency and few-turn tool usage, and general reasoning tasks under strict time cost constraints. Across these tasks, we aim to assess whether models can understand of the concept of "time" under varying limits, and whether they can utilize this awareness to dynamically adjust their reasoning strategies accordingly.
To summarize, our contributions are as follows:
\begin{itemize}
    \item We introduce the concept of the Timely Machine, redefining "test time compute" in agentic scenarios as the physical quantity of time itself. This conceptual shift offers a potential reference for future research.
    \item We propose Timely-Eval, the first benchmark designed to verify tool invocation capabilities under varying time cost constraints. Through extensive evaluations of state-of-the-art models, we observe that [+findings].
    \item We develop Timely-RL, a reinforcement learning approach that endows models with an accurate perception of time. We demonstrate that Timely-RL effectively enables even smaller models to achieve  [+results].
\end{itemize}

# Abstract

As Large Language Models (LLMs) increasingly leverage external tools and environmental feedback for complex reasoning tasks, the traditional notion of test-time compute—defined by generation length—becomes inadequate. In agentic scenarios with unpredictable tool execution latencies, generation length no longer reliably correlates with actual task completion time, rendering conventional test-time scaling strategies ineffective. We introduce the **Timely Machine**, a paradigm that redefines test-time compute as the physical quantity of time itself, rather than token count. We argue that temporal awareness and cost-aware planning should be intrinsic capabilities of LLMs in interactive environments, enabling them to dynamically adjust generation strategies based on real-time constraints and environmental feedback. To evaluate this capability, we present **Timely-Eval**, the first benchmark designed to assess model performance across diverse interactive scenarios under varying time budgets—from high-frequency multi-turn tool invocations to low-frequency interactions and time-constrained reasoning tasks. Furthermore, we develop **Timely-RL**, a reinforcement learning framework that instills accurate time perception in models, demonstrating that even smaller models can effectively adapt their reasoning strategies to optimize performance within fixed temporal constraints. Our work establishes a new foundation for understanding and optimizing test-time compute in the era of agentic AI systems, where the intelligent allocation of actual time, rather than mere token generation, determines task success.

---

**Keywords:** Large Language Models, Test-Time Compute, Agentic AI, Tool Use, Reinforcement Learning, Time-Aware Reasoning
